content:
# John Chapter 19 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 19 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 19:1 — "flogged him" vs. "scourged him" vs. "flogged with a lead-tipped whip"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "scourged [him]" | Literal emastigOsen — scourging |
| LOGOS | "flogged him" | Standard English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "had Jesus flogged with a lead-tipped whip" | Adds "lead-tipped whip" — historical detail not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "flogged." The Greek mastigoO means to flog/scourge. PNEUMA adds the instrument description (a historically accurate detail about Roman flagellum, but not specified in the text).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:2 — "arrayed him in a purple robe" vs. "threw around him a purple garment" vs. "put a purple robe on him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "threw around him a purple garment" | Literal periballon = "threw around"; himation = "garment" |
| LOGOS | "arrayed him in a purple robe" | "Arrayed" — dignified language; "robe" |
| PNEUMA | "put a purple robe on him" | Simple English |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "arrayed." The word choice adds a note of mock-royal ceremony appropriate to the scene.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor stylistic divergence.

---

## 19:3 — "struck him with their hands" vs. "were giving him slaps" vs. "slapped him across the face"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they were giving him slaps" | Literal edidoun autO rapismata — iterative imperfect |
| LOGOS | "struck him with their hands" | General rendering |
| PNEUMA | "they mocked, as they slapped him across the face" | Adds "mocked" and "across the face" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Struck him with their hands" covers rapismata (blows/slaps). PNEUMA adds "mocked" (already conveyed by "Hail, King!") and "across the face" (not in Greek).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:5 — "Behold the man!" vs. "Look, here is the man!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, the man!" | Literal ide ho anthrOpos |
| LOGOS | "Behold the man!" | Same — preserves "Behold" (Ecce homo) |
| PNEUMA | "Look, here is the man!" | "Look" for ide; adds "here is" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "Behold the man!" This is one of the most iconic phrases in the NT (Ecce homo). "Behold" preserves the dignity and strangeness of the moment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "Look, here is" is more colloquial but loses the weightiness.

---

## 19:7 — "he has made himself the Son of God" vs. "he called himself the Son of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he made himself Son of God" | Literal heauton epoiEsen huion theou; no article before "Son" |
| LOGOS | "he has made himself the Son of God" | Adds article for English |
| PNEUMA | "he called himself the Son of God" | "Called" replaces "made" — different Greek verb (poieO vs. kaleO) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek says epoiEsen ("made") not ekalasen ("called"). "Made himself" (the Son of God) is the accusation — self-appointment, not mere verbal claim.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "called" misrepresents the Greek verb.

---

## 19:10 — "authority" vs. "power"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I have authority to release you and I have authority to crucify you" | exousia = "authority" |
| LOGOS | "I have authority to release you and authority to crucify you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I have the power to release you or crucify you" | "Power" for exousia |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "authority." Exousia means delegated authority/right — not raw power (dynamis). The distinction matters in v.11 where Jesus says this authority was "given from above."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:11 — "he who delivered me over to you has the greater sin" vs. "the one who handed me over to you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] having handed me over to you has greater sin" | Literal ho paradous; comparative "greater" |
| LOGOS | "he who delivered me over to you has the greater sin" | "Delivered over" for paradous; article "the greater" |
| PNEUMA | "the one who handed me over to you has the greater sin" | "Handed over" — same meaning |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. All three are close; "delivered over" is slightly more formal and suits the judicial context.

**Status:** Minimal divergence. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:13 — "in Aramaic" vs. "in Hebrew" for HebraistE

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in Hebrew, Gabbatha" | Literal HebraistE — which likely means Aramaic in John's usage |
| LOGOS | "in Aramaic Gabbatha" | Interprets HebraistE as Aramaic (linguistically more accurate) |
| PNEUMA | "in Hebrew, Gabbatha" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "in Aramaic." John uses HebraistE to refer to what is linguistically Aramaic (the common language of Palestine). "Aramaic" is more accurate for modern readers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. This is a recurring translation decision throughout John.

---

## 19:14 — "about the sixth hour" vs. "about noon"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "about [the] sixth hour" | Literal hOs hektE |
| LOGOS | "about the sixth hour" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "about noon" | Converts to modern time reference |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "about the sixth hour." The time reference carries theological weight (when Passover lambs are slaughtered) and "the sixth hour" preserves this connection.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "noon" is accurate but loses the literary/theological resonance.

---

## 19:14 — "Behold your King!" vs. "Look, here is your king!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, the king of you!" | Literal ide ho basileus humOn |
| LOGOS | "Behold your King!" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Look, here is your king!" | Colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "Behold your King!" — parallel to "Behold the man!" in v.5. The repeated "Behold" (ide) creates a structural parallel.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:18 — "There they crucified him" vs. "There they nailed him to the cross"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "where they crucified him" | Literal estaurOsan |
| LOGOS | "There they crucified him" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "There they nailed him to the cross" | "Nailed" specifies the method; not in the verb stauroo |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "crucified." StauroO means "to crucify" — "nailed" specifies a method that the Greek verb does not.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:22 — "What I have written I have written" vs. "No, what I have written, I have written"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What I have written, I have written" | Literal ho gegrapha gegrapha |
| LOGOS | "What I have written I have written" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "No, what I have written, I have written" | Adds "No" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering without "No." The Greek is abrupt and final without a negative particle.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:23 — "tunic" vs. "robe" for chitOn

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the tunic" | chitOn = inner garment |
| LOGOS | "his tunic" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "his robe" | "Robe" — wrong garment; himation is the outer robe, chitOn is the inner tunic |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "tunic." The distinction between himatia (outer garments, divided four ways) and chitOn (inner tunic, seamless) is important. PNEUMA's "robe" confuses the two.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The seamless tunic (not robe) carries high-priestly symbolism.

---

## 19:24 — "cast lots" vs. "threw dice"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "cast [a] lot" | Literal ebalon klEron |
| LOGOS | "cast lots" | Standard biblical rendering |
| PNEUMA | "throw dice" | Modern equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "cast lots." This is a direct quotation from Psalm 22:18, and the traditional biblical language should be preserved in a Scripture quotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:26 — "Woman, behold, your son!" vs. "Dear woman, here is your son"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Woman, behold, the son of you" | Literal gunai, ide |
| LOGOS | "Woman, behold, your son!" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Dear woman, here is your son" | Adds "Dear"; "here is" for "behold" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Woman" (gunai) is not disrespectful in Greek — it is a formal address (cf. 2:4). Adding "Dear" softens what is a solemn, formal declaration. "Behold" preserves the ide that echoes vv.5, 14.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:28 — "all was now finished" vs. "his mission was now finished"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "knowing that already all things have been completed" | Literal panta tetelestai |
| LOGOS | "knowing that all was now finished" | Natural English |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus knew that his mission was now finished" | "Mission" interprets panta ("all things") |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek says "all things" (panta) — not "his mission." "All" is broader and more encompassing.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:29 — "sour wine" vs. "vinegar"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "vinegar" | oxos — can mean sour wine or vinegar |
| LOGOS | "sour wine" | Interprets oxos as posca (Roman soldiers' drink) |
| PNEUMA | "sour wine" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "sour wine." Oxos in this context refers to posca, the cheap sour wine that Roman soldiers drank — not culinary vinegar.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 19:30 — "gave up his spirit" vs. "handed over the spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he handed over the spirit" | Literal paredOken to pneuma — same verb (paradidOmi) used for "handing over" throughout the Passion |
| LOGOS | "gave up his spirit" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "gave up his spirit" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "gave up his spirit." EMET's "handed over" is a fascinating literal reading — the same verb used for Judas's betrayal (paradidOmi) is used for Jesus' voluntary death. This wordplay is noted but the traditional "gave up" is adopted for clarity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's note about the paradidOmi wordplay is valuable for commentary.

---

## 19:31 — "for that Sabbath was a high day" vs. "a very special Sabbath, because it was Passover week"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for that Sabbath was a great [day]" | Literal megalE hE hEmera ekeinou tou sabbatou |
| LOGOS | "for that Sabbath was a high day" | "High day" — traditional rendering for a Sabbath coinciding with a festival |
| PNEUMA | "and a very special Sabbath, because it was Passover week" | "Very special" and adds "because it was Passover week" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "a high day." This is the traditional term for a Sabbath that coincides with a major festival. PNEUMA's explanation, while helpful, adds content not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:35 — Eyewitness testimony rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] having seen has testified, and true is the testimony of him, and that [one] knows that he speaks true things, that you also may believe" | Literal; preserves pronoun ambiguity |
| LOGOS | "He who saw it has borne witness — his testimony is true, and he knows that he is telling the truth — that you also may believe" | Dashes for parenthetical emphasis |
| PNEUMA | "(This report is from an eyewitness giving an accurate account. He speaks the truth so that you also can believe.)" | Parenthetical; "report" replaces "testimony"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Borne witness" (memartyrEken) is the key Johannine term. PNEUMA's "report" and "accurate account" reduce the forensic weight of marturia (testimony/witness).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:39 — "about seventy-five pounds" vs. "about a hundred pounds"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "about a hundred pounds" | Literal hOs litras hekaton — 100 Roman litrai |
| LOGOS | "about seventy-five pounds" | Converts Roman litrai to approximate modern pounds |
| PNEUMA | "about seventy-five pounds" | Same conversion as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "seventy-five pounds." A Roman litra was approximately 12 ounces, so 100 litrai equals roughly 75 modern pounds. The conversion helps modern readers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's literal "hundred pounds" is technically "hundred litrai," which would mislead readers who assume modern pounds.

---

## 19:39 — "a mixture of myrrh and aloes" vs. "perfumed ointment made from myrrh and aloes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a mixture of myrrh and aloes" | Literal migma smurnEs kai aloEs |
| LOGOS | "a mixture of myrrh and aloes" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "perfumed ointment made from myrrh and aloes" | Adds "perfumed ointment" — interpretive expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek says migma (mixture) — not "ointment" or "perfumed."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 19:40 — "linen cloths" vs. "long sheets of linen cloth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bound it in linen cloths" | Literal othoniois — linen strips/cloths |
| LOGOS | "bound it in linen cloths" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "wrapped Jesus' body with the spices in long sheets of linen cloth" | "Long sheets" — specifies shape; "wrapped" for "bound" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "linen cloths." Othonia are linen cloths/wrappings — the text does not specify "long sheets."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adding descriptive content not in Greek | 8 | "lead-tipped whip" (v.1), "across the face" (v.3), "Dear" (v.26), "nailed" (v.18), "No" (v.22), "perfumed ointment" (v.39), "long sheets" (v.40), "Passover week" (v.31) |
| Key term divergence | 4 | exousia: authority/power (v.10), chitOn: tunic/robe (v.23), marturia: testimony/report (v.35), ide: behold/look (vv.5, 14, 26) |
| PNEUMA interpreting broad terms narrowly | 3 | panta: all/mission (v.28), epoiEsen: made/called (v.7), staurOsan: crucified/nailed (v.18) |
| Time/measurement conversions | 2 | Sixth hour/noon (v.14), litrai/pounds (v.39) |
| Structural "Behold" pattern preserved | 3 | vv.5, 14, 26 — LOGOS preserves the ide repetition; PNEUMA varies |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA near-identical | ~37 | Most verses identical |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

John 19 — the crucifixion — is the most sacred text in Christian scripture, and translation decisions here carry ultimate weight. The chapter reveals that PNEUMA's impulse to add vivid physical detail ("lead-tipped whip," "nailed him to the cross," "slapped across the face") sometimes serves the modern reader's imagination but betrays the Greek's deliberate restraint. John's Passion Narrative is understated — the crucifixion itself receives exactly three words (hopou auton estaurOsan, "where they crucified him").

The most critical rendering in the chapter is Tetélestai ("It is finished," v.30), where all three translators agree on the translation but differ in punctuation: LOGOS and SOPHIA use a period (declaration), PNEUMA uses an exclamation point (victory shout). Both are defensible.

The "Behold" (ide) pattern across vv.5, 14, and 26 is a key literary feature that LOGOS preserves and PNEUMA disrupts. "Behold the man!" — "Behold your King!" — "Behold your son!" creates a descending sequence from humanity to kingship to family that structures the entire chapter.

EMET provides a valuable insight on v.30: the verb paredOken (gave up/handed over) is the same verb used throughout the Passion for "betrayal/handing over" — creating a wordplay where Jesus' voluntary death mirrors Judas's betrayal. This is a case where EMET's literal sensitivity reveals a textual connection that both LOGOS and PNEUMA miss.

---
*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — 2026-03-05*
